title: Latest News from Science Magazine
description: Keyword search result for Alerts: News Weekly Headlines -- New results matching your keyword search query (updated daily).

items.title: How bumble bees survive days underwater without drowning
items.description: Insects can stay alive for up to a week in flooded nests, thanks to several tricks

items.title: White House plan to break up iconic U.S. climate lab moves forward
items.description: Bidders have lined up to take over pieces of the National Center for Atmospheric Research

items.title: Ancient Peruvians transported live parrots across the Andes
items.description: Pre-Inca elites wore headdresses made of feathers from Amazonian macaws

items.title: Why do rodents gnaw? Because it feels good
items.description: Newly mapped brain circuitry in mice links tooth grinding to pleasure pathways

items.title: These ants navigate with a newly discovered ‘Moon compass’
items.description: Insects must perform tricky internal calculations to travel in a straight line

items.title: NASA administrator talks to <cite>Science</cite> about studying the Moon, Mars—and Earth
items.description: Jared Isaacman says agency may accelerate lunar science program and could tackle a new Mars mission in 2028

items.title: U.S. agency will devote $144 million to studies that slow aging, extend quality of life
items.description: ARPA-H will “build the train tracks” for first large clinical studies of aging interventions

items.title: Career effects of preprints get mixed reviews from biomedical researchers
items.description: Junior researchers are more likely to embrace preprints; grant reviewers and hiring committees express doubts

items.title: Can a wealthy family change the course of a deadly brain disease?
items.description: Inspired by the loss of their mother, they have poured millions into studying a key protein behind frontotemporal dementia. But all has not gone according to plan

items.title: New HIV cure approach forces hidden virus into tripping immune sensor
items.description: Strategy gains momentum after promising results in cell studies and infected people
